text:
*[Bug 19088] Fix problem in stats::contrast() documentation*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19088>

Sebastian Meyer (<mailto:seb.meyer@fau.de|seb.meyer@fau.de>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
         Resolution|---                         |INVALID
                 CC|                            |seb.meyer@fau.de
             Status|UNCONFIRMED                 |CLOSED

--- Comment #1 from Sebastian Meyer (<mailto:seb.meyer@fau.de|seb.meyer@fau.de>) ---
This is not a bug. 'contrast.Rd' simply uses the `\linkS4class` macro in its
extended form available since R 4.6.0. The corresponding NEWS entry is:

> the \linkS4class macro now also works
> for links to other packages via
> \linkS4class[<pkg>]{<myClass>}, much nicer than the
> "workaround" \link[<pkg>:<myClass>-class]{<myClass>}
> necessary in earlier versions of R.

Packages using the new optional argument require R >= 4.6.0.

Note that copying Rd fragments from other packages is fragile (leaving aside
possible licensing issues). It is much better to `\link` to the original
documentation. You have seen one problem (inheriting a newer R version
dependency) but there are more. For example, all build/install-stage dynamic
help fragments from the source become static in your Rd copy, in the form
evaluated on your build machine. Copied fragments might also refer to other
parts of the original help page (e.g., "see Details") or contain citations that
only make sense in conjunction with the References.


(In reply to Elin Waring from comment #0)
> Created attachment 3730
> patch for stats/man/contrast.Rd

Note that this would not be a valid replacement and give a check warning. See
the above NEWS entry for the back-compatible form.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

                 CC|                            |seb.meyer@fau.de

--- Comment #2 from Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) ---
(In reply to Sebastian Meyer from comment #1)
> This is not a bug. 

Indeed, **not at all**.
If there is a bug it is in roxygen's mis-feature to allow _stealing_ other
developers' documentation which is (by default) copyrighted, without
acknowledging the original author(s) .. as Sebastian already mentioned (less
explicitly).

I strongly agree that people should (if not even **must**) use `\link{.}`
instead.

*[Bug 19083] setGeneric("f", \(x) { standardGeneric("x") }) makes generic with class 'nonstandardGenericFunction'*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19083>

--- Comment #1 from Michael Chirico (<mailto:michaelchirico4@gmail.com|michaelchirico4@gmail.com>) ---
Just looked at this again and noticed that I switched the examples around, it
should read as:

 - `body()` retains srcref attributes for braced functions, but not unbraced
ones:


```
      options(keep.source=TRUE) # force for batch sessions
      is.null(attributes(body(function(x) x)))
      # [1] TRUE
      is.null(attributes(body(function(x) {x} )))
      # [1] FALSE
```

 - `identical(ignore.srcref=TRUE)` does not apply to the `body()` of a closure,
only the closure itself:

```
      options(keep.source=TRUE)
      f1 = function(x) { x }
      f2 = function(x) {
        x
      }
      identical(f1, f2)
      # [1] TRUE
      identical(body(f1), body(f2))
      # [1] FALSE
```

elements.text: [Bug 19083] setGeneric("f", \(x) { standardGeneric("x") }) makes generic with class 'nonstandardGenericFunction'

wrong as html.  The code tag should be wrapped around text only, not the <a>. 
As I said there are a few ways to change it, and I think removing the \code
might be better.

  --> <https://bugs.r-project.org/attachment.cgi?id=3731&action=edit>
Different way of addressing the issue

The issue is *not* invalid, it's just that the approach of the patch was wrong.
This should work.

For better or worse we don't expect base R documentation to meet CRAN
standards, but, in this specific case, not doing so has downstream
consequences.

text:
*[Bug 19088] Fix problem in stats::contrast() documentation*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19088>

--- Comment #5 from Sebastian Meyer (<mailto:seb.meyer@fau.de|seb.meyer@fau.de>) ---
Please note that the 'checkRd' message "Tag LIST is invalid in a \code block"
you are referring to is a consequence of the parsing error "unexpected TEXT
'[Matrix]', expecting '{'", i.e., R < 4.6.0 does not know about the optional
argument for `\linkS4class`. There is nothing wrong with
`\code{\linkS4class[Matrix]{dgCMatrix}}` in R 4.6.0, but **this syntax is
unsupported in R 4.5.3**:

```
R-4.5.3> tools::parse_Rd(textConnection(r"(\linkS4class[Matrix]{dgCMatrix})"),
fragment=TRUE)
[Matrix]{dgCMatrix}
Warning message:
In tools::parse_Rd(textConnection("\\linkS4class[Matrix]{dgCMatrix}"),  :
  <connection>:1: unexpected TEXT '[Matrix]', expecting '{'
```

There is no need for a change in 'contrast.Rd'. What 'bayestestR' should do is
to `\link[stats]{contrasts}` from its help, not copy ("inherit") the deparsed
Rd from the maintainer's R 4.6.0 installation that uses syntax requiring R
4.6.0.

text:
*[Bug 19087] Security issue with `pdf()`/`postscript()`*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19087>

Elin Waring (<mailto:elin.waring@gmail.com|elin.waring@gmail.com>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |elin.waring@gmail.com

--- Comment #1 from Elin Waring (<mailto:elin.waring@gmail.com|elin.waring@gmail.com>) ---
This seems like an important issue. Yes, developers should always sanitize
their inputs, but the question is whether a bad actor could use this in a very
destructive way. PDFs are dangerous anyway because of how malevolent code can
be embedded in them.
<https://github.com/luigigubello/PayloadsAllThePDFs>

From what is being said, anyone can use this option to craft a dangerous PDF
using R. 

I'm curious to know what the packages using this construct are doing so. Is
there a problem it solves for them?

elements.text:
) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |elin.waring@gmail.com

) ---

there a problem it solves for them?

text:
*[Bug 19087] Security issue with `pdf()`/`postscript()`*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19087>

Simon Urbanek (<mailto:simon.urbanek@r-project.org|simon.urbanek@r-project.org>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |simon.urbanek@r-project.org

--- Comment #2 from Simon Urbanek (<mailto:simon.urbanek@r-project.org|simon.urbanek@r-project.org>) ---
That main valid use is `pdf("|cat")` which allows output to stdout without
resorting to temporary files. There is no good/easy replacement for that
use-case if this functionality is removed, so it would require some additional
argument or similar handling.

I disagree that it's important, because anything passed to such I/O functions
**must** be validated regardless, not doing so is not only very bad practice,
but it is inherently dangerous - even other characters like `/` can be used for
an attack on other parts of the FS if not sanitized, so the pipe is not the
only worry. Therefore removing support for the pipe would reduce the attack
surface only slightly, it is no substitute for making sure all API/REST inputs
are validated before use.

I think one possible alternative solution would be to add an argument that
toggles this functionality with the default changing to `FALSE`. It would also
be a lot easier since it could simply raise an error if a pipe is used in the
name when it is not allowed instead of changing the actual implementation.

elements.text:

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |simon.urbanek@r-project.org

text:
*[Bug 19088] Fix problem in stats::contrast() documentation*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19088>

--- Comment #6 from Elin Waring (<mailto:elin.waring@gmail.com|elin.waring@gmail.com>) ---
No, we're talking about failing builds on CRAN, but I guess at some point
everyone that is happening to will get notified and required to update.  I
looked at a number of md files with `\linkS4class` and most of them wrap in
\code although not necessarily consistently 
I guess do that or require a 4.6 minimum to avoid the older CRAN builds. In
other words, it's a breaking change.

I'll just leave here for when other people start getting the same issue from
CRAN.

<https://search.r-project.org/R/refmans/stats/html/contrasts.html>

This is the value parameter description.


> either a numeric matrix (or a sparse or dense matrix of a class extending dMatrix > (pkg Matrix) whose columns give coefficients for contrasts in the levels of x, or > (the quoted name of) a function which computes such matrices.


Generates this html for the link to Matrix as a package is

```
<a href="https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=Matrix"><span
class="pkg">Matrix</span></a>
```

For the link to the class it is

```
<code><a href="../../Matrix/help/dgCMatrix-class.html">dgCMatrix</a></code>
```

CRAN does not like having the <code></code> tag _outside_ of the <a></a>
because it is poorly formed HTML.   CRAN likes the link to Matrix because the
<span></span> because the span wraps the text only (that is, it is _inside_ the
<a></a>. 

So for anyone seeing this issue 

Meanwhile, for the particular package this came yo for, I'll send them a patch.
